summary: Image A is more effective at grabbing the viewer's attention and making them focus on the spatial layout and visual details. Image B, however, triggers a stronger emotional response and feels more rewarding or pleasing to the viewer.

detailed.winner_reason: Apple-style clean design triggers stronger visual processing and spatial attention, making it easier for the brain to process and remember.
detailed.emotional_impact: Image B (cluttered store) triggers a stronger emotional response in the orbitofrontal cortex, but this is likely frustration or overwhelm rather than positive engagement. Image A creates a calmer, more rewarding viewing experience.
detailed.visual_attention: Image A captures focused attention through the superior parietal cortex, directing the eye to key elements. Image B scatters attention across many competing elements, reducing dwell time on any single item.
detailed.memory_retention: The clean design is significantly more memorable. The parahippocampal gyrus shows stronger activation for Image A, meaning viewers will recall the spatial layout and key messages better.
recommendations: Reduce the number of competing visual elements to 3-5 key items per view
Use white space strategically to guide the eye toward your primary call-to-action
Place the most important message where the brain naturally looks first (top-left for Western audiences)
